title: Pastel de nata
subtitle: Portuguese custard tarts with a crisp rough puff shell and scorched egg custard
excerpt: The Portuguese egg custard tart made with a thin, multi-layered rough puff pastry shell and a rich egg yolk and cream custard. Baked at the highest oven temperature possible, the surface of the custard scorches and blisters to a deep amber in patches. Eaten warm, dusted with cinnamon and icing sugar. Originally from the Jeronimos Monastery in Lisbon. The high heat is essential and non-negotiable.
sourceNotes: Pasteis de nata originate from the Jeronimos Monastery in Belem, Lisbon, where monks are believed to have made them from the eighteenth century using surplus egg yolks. The recipe was sold to a local pastry shop in 1837 that still operates. The general formula of laminated pastry, egg yolk custard, and high-heat baking is documented in Portuguese cookery literature and consistent across public domain sources. The scorching of the custard surface requires an oven temperature not achievable with domestic ovens below 240 to 250°C.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked tarts for up to 1 month. Reheat from frozen in a 200°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rough puff pastry can be made a day ahead. The custard can be made several hours ahead and kept chilled. Fill the cases just before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: set oven to its maximum temperature (250°C or as high as it will go). Fan at 230°C. The very high temperature is essential: the custard must scorch and blister in patches on the surface. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the custard surface has dark caramelised spots.

recipeTools.notes: 12-hole; grease each hole generously with softened butter

glossaryTerms.term: High heat for pastel de nata
glossaryTerms.definition: The scorching of the custard surface at 250°C or above is not decorative: it is integral to the flavour and texture of pastel de nata. The very high heat caramelises the surface sugars in seconds to create a bitter, smoky contrast against the sweet custard beneath. At lower temperatures the custard sets without scorching and the result is a plain egg tart, pleasant but lacking the characteristic flavour. The pastry also needs maximum heat to puff and crisp in the very short bake time. If your oven does not reach 250°C, use the highest possible setting and accept that the result will be lighter-coloured.

content.text: The single most important rule: preheat the oven to its absolute maximum for at least 30 minutes before baking.

attrs.term: High heat for pastel de nata

content.text: defines the flavour of a genuine pastel de nata. Bake these on the highest shelf of the oven and watch them: they go from perfect to overdone in under a minute.

attrs.prepNote: cold, diced, for the rough puff pastry

content.text: Make the rough puff: mix the flour and salt. Add the cold diced butter and rub it in roughly, leaving visible flat flakes of butter. Add just enough cold water (about 100 to 125ml) to bring it to a rough dough. Roll out into a rectangle, fold it into thirds, turn 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat twice more for 3 folds total. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Make the custard: whisk the cornflour into a little of the cold milk until smooth. Warm the remaining milk, cream, sugar, lemon zest, and vanilla in a saucepan over medium heat until just steaming. Pour into the cornflour mixture, whisk, return to the pan, and cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until thick enough to coat the spoon. Remove from the heat, whisk in the egg yolks one at a time. Strain through a sieve into a jug. Cover the surface with cling film and leave to cool.

Preheat the oven to 250°C (or maximum) for at least 30 minutes. Grease each hole in the muffin tin generously with softened butter. Roll the chilled pastry as thin as possible on a lightly floured surface and cut into 12 circles large enough to line each hole. Press each circle firmly into the tin, working it up the sides. Fill each case two-thirds full with custard (it will expand during baking). Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the custard surface has dark scorched patches. Transfer to a rack immediately. Dust with cinnamon and icing sugar and eat warm.
